task: I’ve got a site incident that needs to be handled before the end of the shift, and there are a few people asking for updates. What should I make sure gets recorded or passed along first so the next steps stay organized and nothing important gets missed?

turns.agent_response:
First, freeze the issue and get the basics written down **today** so the story doesn’t drift.

Make sure the update includes:

- **What was happening at the time** the incident occurred
- **Exact location** on the site
- **Who was involved** and **who said/did what**
- **What work was ongoing** right then
- **Who gave the instruction or approval**, if any
- **What was damaged, disturbed, or put at risk**
- **Whether the foreman, superintendent, inspector, or owner rep was informed**
- **Who witnessed it**, with **names and phone numbers**
- **What immediate action was taken** to secure or stop the issue
- **What still needs to be done** and **who owns the next step**

If it’s anything more than a simple fix, get the **official side involved the same day** so it doesn’t turn into loose talk, changing stories, or a buried problem.

For the people asking for updates, keep it short and consistent:
- what happened
- whether it’s contained
- who has been notified
- what the next step is
- when the next update will come

If you want, I can turn this into a **quick end-of-shift incident note template** you can fill in fast.

user_simulator.text:
Good start, but I’d want it tighter and more jobsite-useful. Needs the **time**, **date**, **exact spot**, **what crew/trade was active**, and **whether work was stopped**. Also record **photos/video**, **equipment involved**, **weather/conditions if it matters**, and the **paper trail** — who was called, when, and what they said.

For updates, I’d keep one version going out so nobody freelances the story. Short, plain, same facts every time. No guessing, no opinions.

If you’ve got a real incident, I’d also want a simple **line for cost impact / delay** and whether it’s going to hit inspection, delivery, or a follow-up crew. That’s what bites you later.

turns.agent_response:
Exactly — for end-of-shift handling, I’d lock in these items first so the record stays clean and usable:

### Record immediately
- **Date and exact time**
- **Exact location** on the site
- **What crew/trade was active**
- **What was happening at the moment**
- **Whether work was stopped** and by whom
- **Who was involved** and **who said/did what**
- **Equipment/materials involved**
- **Photos/video taken** before anything is moved or changed
- **Weather/conditions** if they matter
- **Who was called/notified, when, and what they said**
- **What was damaged, disturbed, exposed, or put at risk**
- **Who witnessed it** with names and phone numbers
- **What immediate containment or correction was done**
- **Any cost impact, delay, inspection issue, delivery issue, or follow-up crew impact**
- **What still needs to happen** and **who owns it**

### Pass along first
Keep the update to one consistent version:
- what happened
- where and when it happened
- whether it’s contained
- whether work stopped
- who has been informed
- what the next step is
- when the next update will come

### Best practice
- **No guessing, no opinions, no side commentary**
- **Same facts every time**
- **Same-day documentation**
- If it’s more than a minor issue, **get the foreman/superintendent/inspector/owner rep involved the same day**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-minute site incident update template** you can paste into a text or report.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the right list. Tight, usable, no fluff.

I’d run it like this every time:
- date and exact time
- exact spot
- crew/trade on it
- what was happening when it went sideways
- whether work got stopped, and who stopped it
- who was involved
- equipment/materials involved
- photos/video before anything gets touched
- weather/conditions if they matter
- who got called, when, and what they said
- what was damaged or put at risk
- witnesses with names and numbers
- what got done right then to contain it
- cost hit / delay / inspection / delivery / follow-on crew impact
- what still needs to happen and who owns it

And for updates, same story every time. One version only. No freelancing, no opinions.

That’s the part people screw up — they start talking around it instead of pinning it down while it’s fresh.
